volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Hippolytus (Section 2.151 - Section 311)

sections.label: Section 2.151
sections.original: — ἢ πόσιν , τὸν Ἐρεχθειδᾶν
sections.translation: Or doth some rival in thy house beguile thy lord , the captain of Erechtheus ’ sons , that hero nobly bom , to secret amours hid from thee ?

sections.label: Section 2.155
sections.original: — ἢ ναυβάτας τις ἔπλευσεν Κρή-
sections.translation: Or hath some mariner sailing hither from Crete reached this port that sailors love , with evil tidings for our queen , and she with sorrow for her grievous fate

sections.label: Section 2.160
sections.original: εὐναία δέδεται ψυχά ;
sections.translation: is to her bed confined ?

sections.label: Section 161
sections.original: — φιλεῖ δὲ τᾷ δυστρόπῳ γυναικῶν
sections.translation: Yea , and oft o ’ er woman ’ s wayward nature settles a feeling of miserable perplexity , arising from labour-pains or passionate desire .

sections.label: Section 165
sections.original: δι ’ ἐμᾶς ᾖξέν ποτε νηδύος ἅδ ’
sections.translation: I , too , have felt at times this sharp thrill shoot through me , but I would cry to Artemis , queen of archery , who comes from heaven to aid us in our travail , and thanks to heaven ’ s grace she ever comes at my call with welcome help .

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: — ἀλλ ’ ἥδε τροφὸς γεραιὰ πρὸ θυρῶν
sections.translation: Look ! where the aged nurse is bringing her forth from the house before the door , while on her brow the cloud of gloom is deepening . My soul longs to learn what is her grief , the canker that is

sections.label: Section 175
sections.original: δέμας ἀλλόχροον βασιλείας .
sections.translation: wasting our queen ’ s fading charms .

sections.label: Section 176
sections.original: ὦ κακὰ θνητῶν στυγεραί τε νόσοι .
sections.translation: O , the ills of mortal men ! the cruel diseases they endure ! What can I do for thee ? from what refrain ? Here is the bright sun-light , here the azure sky ; lo ! we have brought thee on thy bed of sickness

sections.label: Section 180
sections.original: δέμνια κοίτης .
sections.translation: without the palace ; for all thy talk was of coming hither , but soon back to thy chamber wilt thou hurry . Disappointment follows fast with thee , thou hast no joy in aught for long ; the present has no power to please ; on something absent

sections.label: Section 185
sections.original: φίλτερον ἡγῇ .
sections.translation: next thy heart is set . Better be sick than tend the sick ; the first is but a single ill , the last unites mental grief with manual toil . Man ’ s whole life is full of anguish ;

sections.label: Section 190
sections.original: κοὐκ ἔστι πόνων ἀνάπαυσις .
sections.translation: no respite from his woes he finds ; but if there is aught to love beyond this life , night ’ s dark pall doth wrap it round . And so we show our mad love of this life because its light is shed on earth ,

sections.label: Section 195
sections.original: δι ’ ἀπειροσύνην ἄλλου βιότου
sections.translation: and because we know no other , and have naught revealed to us of all our earth may hide ; and trusting to fables we drift at random .

sections.label: Section 198
sections.original: ἄρατέ μου δέμας , ὀρθοῦτε κάρα ·
sections.translation: Lift my body , raise my head ! My limbs are all unstrung , kind friends .

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: λάβετ ’ εὐπήχεις χεῖρας , πρόπολοι .
sections.translation: O handmaids , lift my arms , my shapely arms . The tire on my head is too heavy for me to wear ; away with it , and let my tresses o ’ er my shoulders fall .

sections.label: Section 203
sections.original: θάρσει , τέκνον , καὶ μὴ χαλεπῶς
sections.translation: Be of good heart , dear child ; toss not so wildly to and fro .

sections.label: Section 205
sections.original: ῥᾷον δὲ νόσον μετά θ ’ ἡσυχίας
sections.translation: Lie still , be brave , so wilt thou find thy sickness easier to bear ; suffering for mortals is nature ’ s iron law .

sections.label: Section 208
sections.original: αἰαῖ ·
sections.translation: Ah ! would I could draw a draught of water pure from some dew-fed spring , and lay me down to rest in the grassy meadow ’ neath the poplar ’ s shade !

sections.label: Section 212
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , τί θροεῖς ;
sections.translation: My child , what wild speech is this ? O say not such things in public , wild whirling words of frenzy bred !

sections.label: Section 215
sections.original: πέμπετέ μ ’ εἰς ὄρος · εἶμι πρὸς ὕλαν
sections.translation: Away to the mountain take me ! to the wood , to the pine-trees I will go , where hounds pursue the prey , hard on the scent of dappled fawns . Ye gods ! what joy to hark them on ,

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: καὶ παρὰ χαίταν ξανθὰν ῥῖψαι
sections.translation: to grasp the barbed dart , to poise Thessalian hunting-spears close to my golden hair , then let them fly .

sections.label: Section 223
sections.original: τί ποτ ’, ὦ τέκνον , τάδε κηραίνεις ;
sections.translation: Why , why , my child , these anxious cares ? What hast thou to do with the chase ?

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: τί δὲ κρηναίων νασμῶν ἔρασαι ;
sections.translation: Why so eager for the flowing spring , when hard by these towers stands a hill well watered , whence thou may ’ st freely draw ?

sections.label: Section 228
sections.original: δέσποιν ’ ἁλίας Ἄρτεμι Λίμνας
sections.translation: O Artemis , who watchest o ’ er sea-beat Limna [*] and the race-course thundering to the horse ’ s hoofs ,

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: εἴθε γενοίμαν ἐν σοῖς δαπέδοις ,
sections.translation: would I were upon thy plains curbing Venetian steeds !

sections.label: Section 232
sections.original: τί τόδ ’ αὖ παράφρων ἔρριψας ἔπος ;
sections.translation: Why betray thy frenzy in these wild whirling words ? Now thou wert for hasting hence to the hills away to hunt wild beasts , and now

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: ἐπ ’ ἀκυμάντοις πώλων ἔρασαι .
sections.translation: thy yearning is to drive the steed over the waveless sands . This needs a cunning seer to say what god it is that reins thee from the course , distracting thy senses , child .

sections.label: Section 239
sections.original: δύστηνος ἐγώ , τί ποτ ’ εἰργασάμην ;
sections.translation: Ah me ! alas ! what have I done ?

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: ποῖ παρεπλάγχθην γνώμης ἀγαθῆς ;
sections.translation: Whither have I strayed , my senses leaving ? Mad , mad ! stricken by some demon ’ s curse ! Woe is me ! Cover my head again , nurse . Shame fills me for the words I have spoken .

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: κρύπτε · κατ ’ ὄσσων δάκρυ μοι βαίνει ,
sections.translation: Hide me then ; from my eyes the tear-drops stream , and for very shame I turn them away . Tis painful coming to one ’ s senses again , and madness , evil though it be , has this advantage , that one has no knowledge of reason ’ s overthrow .

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: κρύπτω · τὸ δ ’ ἐμὸν πότε δὴ θάνατος
sections.translation: There then I cover thee ; but when will death hide my body in the grave ? Many a lesson length of days is teaching me . Yea , mortal men should pledge themselves

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: καὶ μὴ πρὸς ἄκρον μυελὸν ψυχῆς ,
sections.translation: to moderate friendships only , not to such as reach the very heart ’ s core ; affection ’ s ties should be light upon them to let them slip or draw them tight . For one poor heart to grieve for twain , as I do

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: τῆσδ ’ ὑπεραλγῶ .
sections.translation: for my mistress , is a burden sore to bear . Men say that too engrossing pursuits in life more oft cause disappointment than pleasure ,

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: τοῦ μηδὲν ἄγαν ·
sections.translation: and too oft are foes to health . Wherefore I do not praise excess so much as moderation , and with me wise men will agree .

sections.label: Section 267
sections.original: γύναι γεραιά , βασιλίδος πιστὴ τροφὲ
sections.translation: O aged dame , faithful nurse of Phaedra , our queen , we see her sorry plight ; but what it is that ails her we cannot discern ,

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: σοῦ δ ’ ἂν πυθέσθαι καὶ κλύειν βουλοίμεθ ’ ἄν .
sections.translation: so fain would learn of thee and hear thy opinion .

sections.label: Section 271
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδ ’ ἐλέγχους · οὐ γὰρ ἐννέπειν θέλει .
sections.translation: I question her , but am no wiser , for she will not answer .

sections.label: Section 272
sections.original: οὐδ ’ ἥτις ἀρχὴ τῶνδε πημάτων ἔφυ ;
sections.translation: Nor tell what source these sorrows have ?

sections.label: Section 273
sections.original: ἐς ταὐτὸν ἥκεις · πάντα γὰρ σιγᾷ τάδε .
sections.translation: The same answer thou must take , for she is dumb on every point .

sections.label: Section 274
sections.original: ὡς ἀσθενεῖ τε καὶ κατέξανται δέμας .
sections.translation: How weak and wasted is her body !

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: πῶς δ ’ οὔ , τριταίαν γ ’ οὖσ ’ ἄσιτος ἡμέραν ;
sections.translation: What marvel ? ’ tis three days now since she has tasted food .

sections.label: Section 276
sections.original: πότερον ὑπ ’ ἄτης ἢ θανεῖν πειρωμένη ;
sections.translation: Is this infatuation , or an attempt to die ?

sections.label: Section 277
sections.original: θανεῖν ; ἀσιτεῖ γ ’ εἰς ἀπόστασιν βίου .
sections.translation: ’ Tis death she courts ; such fasting aims at ending life .

sections.label: Section 278
sections.original: θαυμαστὸν εἶπας , εἰ τάδ ’ ἐξαρκεῖ πόσει .
sections.translation: A strange story ! is her husband satisfied ?

sections.label: Section 279
sections.original: κρύπτει γὰρ ἥδε πῆμα κοὔ φησιν νοσεῖν .
sections.translation: She hides from him her sorrow , and vows she is not ill .

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: ὃ δ ’ ἐς πρόσωπον οὐ τεκμαίρεται βλέπων ;
sections.translation: Can he not guess it from her face ?

sections.label: Section 281
sections.original: ἔκδημος ὢν γὰρ τῆσδε τυγχάνει χθονός .
sections.translation: He is not now in his own country .

sections.label: Section 282
sections.original: σὺ δ ’ οὐκ ἀνάγκην προσφέρεις , πειρωμένη
sections.translation: But dost not thou insist in thy endeavour to find out her complaint , her crazy mind ?

sections.label: Section 284
sections.original: ἐς πάντ ’ ἀφῖγμαι κοὐδὲν εἴργασμαι πλέον ·
sections.translation: I have tried every plan , and all in vain ;

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: οὐ μὴν ἀνήσω γ ’ οὐδὲ νῦν προθυμίας ,
sections.translation: yet not even now will I relax my zeal , that thou too , if thou stayest , mayst witness my devotion to my unhappy mistress .

sections.label: Section 288
sections.original: ἄγ ’, ὦ φίλη παῖ , τῶν πάροιθε μὲν λόγων
sections.translation: Come , come , my darling child , let us forget , the twain of us , our former words ; be thou more mild ,

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: στυγνὴν ὀφρὺν λύσασα καὶ γνώμης ὁδόν ,
sections.translation: smoothing that sullen brow and changing the current of thy thought , and I , if in aught before I failed in humouring thee , will let that be and find some better course . If thou art sick with ills thou canst not name , there be women here to help to set thee right ;

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: εἰ δ ’ ἔκφορός σοι συμφορὰ πρὸς ἄρσενας ,
sections.translation: but if thy trouble can to men ’ s ears be divulged , speak , that physicians may pronounce on it .

sections.label: Section 297
sections.original: εἶἑν · τί σιγᾷς ; οὐκ ἐχρῆν σιγᾶν , τέκνον ,
sections.translation: Come , then , why so dumb ? Thou shouldst not so remain , my child , but scold me if I speak amiss , or , if I give good counsel , yield assent .

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: φθέγξαι τι , δεῦρ ’ ἄθρησον . — ὦ τάλαιν ’ ἐγώ ,
sections.translation: One word , one look this way ! Ah me ! Friends , we waste our toil to no purpose ; we are as far away as ever ; she would not relent to my arguments then , nor is she yielding now .

sections.label: Section 304
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ ἴσθι μέντοι — πρὸς τάδ ’ αὐθαδεστέρα
sections.translation: Well , grow more stubborn than the sea , yet be assured of this ,

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: γίγνου θαλάσσης — εἰ θανῇ , προδοῦσα σοὺς
sections.translation: that if thou diest thou art a traitress to thy children , for they will ne ’ er inherit their father ’ s halls , nay , by that knightly queen the Amazon [*] who bore a son to lord it over thine , a bastard born but not a bastard bred , whom well thou knowest ,

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: Ἱππόλυτον
sections.translation: e ’ en Hippolytus .

sections.label: Section 310b
sections.original: οἴμοι .
sections.translation: Oh ! oh !

sections.label: Section 310c
sections.original: θιγγάνει σέθεν τόδε ;
sections.translation: Ha ! doth that touch the quick ?

sections.label: Section 311
sections.original: ἀπώλεσάς με , μαῖα , καί σε πρὸς θεῶν
sections.translation: Thou hast undone me , nurse ; I do adjure by the gods , mention that man no more .
